item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, Obama.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like this skip! Gonna start ctrl-finding "amicus" in every TOS now, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now you shit yourself

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, I reject the clause to go fuck myself.

What you gonna do now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
only facing difficulties due to new instances being created somewhere by a GOI.

This is why it's Keter. They're actively coming from an unknown source. The SCP is effectively not under total containment; not only do you have to keep watch under the instances you gave already, but you need to be on a constant lookout to find more whenever they show up. Once the source has been dealt with and and the production of new instances cease, then it becomes Euclid or Safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a very good skip, I have only some relatively minor issues with it.

It could be classed Euclid. Seriously, there is nothing making it uncontainable or particularly hard to contain. The fact that there is people making new instances of it doesn't render the extant instances harder to deal with. There is nothing particularly hard in the containment of -As, either. They're spread in numbers, but also clustered in few high-density communities. In fact, the article makes it very clear that the current scheme for vigilance and containment is working well enough, only facing difficulties due to new instances being created somewhere by a GOI.

And some huge chunks of the text are more confusing than necessary. Not hugely bad, but it deserves some overall revision, big enough to need the author or authorization, but not really changing the content.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But you explicitly have to be able to read and understand it, even if you don't actually do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Since you're still around and nobody has answered this yet, it was stated that the Foundation was monitoring individuals who have agreed to the contract, so basically the same way they tested everyone else or how people figure out smoking kills- They're not testing it, they're just examining the victims.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Certain classes of individuals (including mentally incapable individuals, pre-pubescent children, and slaves) are unaffected even if they agree

…I don't want to know how they tested that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Clause 217: "By reading this clause, members of any organizations listed in Appendix K.iv agree to go fuck themselves."

I was gonna upvote anyway, but that's just the icing on the cake.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is absolutely fantastic.

If I may ask, where did you get the image? I really like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're a symbol of Amicus's brand of justice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love it. We should really think about what we're agreeing to when we hit the "I Accept" button, but nobody does.

Not suggesting this as a cross-test or anything so foolish, but I only know of one other legal skip, and it's SCP-2553. It can amend contracts… now wouldn't that be an interesting fight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The nooses and hangings feel random and like they have nothing to do with anything. You can't relate it to prisoners because that would imply that the infected are worshiping prisoners, which is hypocritical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The two stand-out bits for me where "the contract's effects do not extend to D-Class personnel" and "Thanks, Obama". The rest is, for the most part, unexceptionally well-done. I could do without the mass-suicide-by-negligence bit, though. +1 on the whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it right up until the "prayer to death" bit. It came a bit too close to the "crazy to death" cliche.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done, but where is the generic magic item? The Thing What Kills You? The badly worded interview, the painfully thin backstory? The 10x10x10 containment cell?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of Third Party in the very best ways, I adore skips like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also also: this has limited potential to spread in areas with low literacy rates, so I would think that 2221-A would encourage teaching literacy.

You don't have to read it; you just have to agree to the contract.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2221-A populations tend to be densely clustered,

I think the description should go into a least a little detail on how the clustering happens.

POP-044-2221-A distributes print versions of SCP-2221, thereby converting nearly all adult members of affected communities into instances of 2221-A.

If the three anomaly-causing clauses can be put into printed documents which get signed, then the description shouldn't say "An instance of SCP-2221 is an End User License Agreement (EULA)". Perhaps you mean to imply one or both of the following:

  1. That for any given infected area, the infection always starts via the EULA vector.
  2. That the modification of EULAs appear to happen spontaneously, without the intervention of any instances of 2221-A.

If so, you should make that explicit.

Also, if this affects EULA for downloaded software, why not also the terms of agreement for signing up on websites?

Also also: this has limited potential to spread in areas with low literacy rates, so I would think that 2221-A would encourage teaching literacy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They publish open letters on the Internet, so kinda. They're basically the Foundation's Wikileaks— disgruntled former and current Foundation personnel who think the public deserves to know the truth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't it be The Republic of Email, now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It follows the law of the Serene Tribunal, not the governments of man. Does the Serene Tribunal have a bit of a conflict of interest? Maybe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hurf, hoh, I am not so smart sometimes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "The imman has asked that all good Muslims sign this petition in protest of the dictator's persecution of the faithful, inshallah."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the concept behind this skip. A thought though, if the mechanics behind the skip are such that it follows govermental law regarding who can be legally bound by it, would the foundation be able to combat it by having governments pass laws declaring that EULAs are no longer legally binding?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this because A) no one reads EULAs, way to capitalize on that, B) 'immune' to D-class testing, that's very clever.

POP-044-2221-A distributes print versions of SCP-2221, thereby converting nearly all adult members of affected communities into instances of 2221-A.

How could they agree to it, though?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Foundationverse thing. There aren't any articles about them yet, but then again, there weren't any articles about the Friends of Amicus yet when they came up in SCP-2310 either. That'll change.

Also, it's not. I was kinda surprised when I searched around and saw nobody had done the evil-EULA thing yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Republic of Letters is still a thing? I thought it basically went out of fashion in the late 18th century (presumably because the sort of person that would participate now could actually start a revolution instead). Or is this a Foundation-verse thing?

Edit: Also is this a rewrite of that deleted EULA based skip?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This might make me one of the dullest women, given how much I enjoy this one. And how terrifying it is. I'm gonna blame lukebn for any resulting lack of sleep that occurs from this.

One mark of advice, remove the tags and let the tagging team handle it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My favorite kind of skip, one dealing in geopolitics, with some tort law as well1. And a very interesting and well-executed one as well.
+1

Footnotes
1. I may very well be the dullest man alive.
